nativeRange.habitat: Smaller tributaries, backwaters, oxbows, and marginal lakes with slow-moving or still water; often near dense vegetation and over soft, muddy substrate.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (essential for barbel protection)
driftwood and leaf litter for cover
smooth rounded substrate where sand not available

diet.notes: Foraging omnivore; sinking pellets, algae wafers, frozen/live bloodworm (chironomid larvae), Tubifex, daphnia, and brine shrimp. Should receive targeted bottom-feeder feeding and not be relied upon to survive on leftovers from tankmates above. Insect larvae, worms, and small crustaceans in the wild.

avoidWith: fish small enough to be startled by their size
boisterous fast-moving species that compete for bottom food

imageCandidates.notes: Standard FishBase species image for Brochis splendens / Corydoras splendens. Check availability and license before use.

summary: The Emerald Cory (Brochis splendens) is an armored catfish prized for its brilliant green-blue iridescence. Larger than typical Corydoras (~7–8 cm), it is traded under both Brochis splendens (current accepted name) and Corydoras splendens (long-standing trade synonym). A strong schooling species; keep in groups of 6 or more. See also fw-fish-177 (Emerald Green Cory — same species, parallel entry).
careNotes:
TAXONOMIC NOTE — Dual-entry species: This entry (fw-fish-175, 'Emerald Cory') and fw-fish-177 ('Emerald Green Cory') describe the same fish and will be merged in end-of-slice cleanup. The accepted scientific name as of 2024 is Brochis splendens (Castelnau 1855); the long-standing aquarium trade synonym Corydoras splendens remains in widespread use and appears on most retail labels and older care guides. A 2003 revision by Britto synonymized Brochis into Corydoras, but the 2024 Dias/Britto Hoplisoma revision restored Brochis as a valid separate genus — so current taxonomy is Brochis splendens. Additional historical synonyms include Callichthys splendens (original description), Brochis dipterus, and Corydoras semiscutatus.

The Emerald Cory is noticeably larger than typical Corydoras, reaching 7–8 cm standard length (up to 9.9 cm per FishBase), and has a higher dorsal fin with 10–12 rays compared to typical Corydoras. It exhibits brilliant green-blue metallic iridescence on the flanks. Juveniles may be sold as 'hi-fin cory' due to an enlarged dorsal.

Fine sand is the preferred substrate — coarse gravel damages the sensitive barbels which are essential for foraging. Maintain groups of at least 6–8; this species has a stronger schooling drive than many other corys and shows increased activity and confidence in larger groups. A 120 cm footprint tank is the practical minimum for a proper school. Hardness tolerance is wide (36–268 ppm, approximately gH 2–15), making them adaptable to most community water conditions. They are facultative air-breathers and will periodically dart to the surface — this is normal behavior, not a sign of oxygen deficiency, though good aeration is still recommended. Provide shaded areas and cover; they are not strictly nocturnal but appreciate low-light hiding spots during the day.
breedingNotes: Breeding is possible in captivity with some conditioning. Separate a group of well-fed adults into a dedicated breeding tank with slightly cooler, soft water and strong water movement to simulate seasonal rain triggers. Females are noticeably rounder and heavier-bodied than males. Spawning follows typical Corydoras-style T-position; eggs are fertilized as the female holds them in her pelvic fin basket. Eggs are deposited near the water surface on broad plant leaves or the aquarium glass and can number in the hundreds to over 1,000 per spawn (FishBase records 830–1,100 eggs). Remove eggs to a separate raising vessel with gentle aeration to prevent fungal infection. Fry are relatively large and can accept microworms and baby brine shrimp soon after becoming free-swimming.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras splendens (Brochis splendens)
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided size (70–80 mm SL), temperature (20–28°C), pH (5.0–8.0), hardness (36–268 ppm), tank dimensions (120×45 cm minimum), diet, behavior, native range (Amazon basin; Brazil, Peru, Ecuador, Colombia), substrate requirements, and synonymy (formerly Brochis splendens; historical synonyms include Callichthys splendens, Brochis dipterus, Corydoras semiscutatus). Note: SeriouslyFish lists the accepted name as Corydoras splendens following Britto 2003, but 2024 Dias/Britto revision restores Brochis — this entry uses Brochis splendens as the current accepted name per the task brief.

additional.name: FishBase — Brochis splendens / Corydoras splendens
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 9.9 cm SL, 40 g), temperature (22–28°C), pH (5.8–8.0), native range (Amazon River basin, South America), habitat (sluggish freshwater with dense vegetation), diet (insect larvae, worms, small crustaceans), taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes, subfamily Corydoradinae), egg count (830–1,100). FishBase carries the species under both Brochis splendens and the synonym Corydoras splendens.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Corydoras Care Guide
additional.notes: General Corydoras guide; confirms Emerald Cory as one of the most popular trade varieties. Notes group size minimum of 6, smooth sand/gravel substrate, temperature 72–82°F (22–28°C), pH 6.5–7.8, tank minimum 20 gallons (76 L) for typical Corydoras but larger recommended for the bigger Brochis/Emerald type. Non-picky feeding behavior confirmed.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Brochis splendens (archived profile)
additional.notes: Earlier SeriouslyFish profile under former genus name; consistent data. Confirmed facultative air-breathing via vascularized intestine, juvenile 'hi-fin cory' trade name, 10–12 dorsal-fin rays as distinguishing feature from Brochis britskii (15–18 rays), and group size recommendation of 6–8 individuals minimum.

fish.breedingNotes: Moderate difficulty; requires conditioning, seasonal trigger (water change + temperature drop), and egg removal. Clutches of 830–1,100 eggs reported. Fry accept microworms and baby brine shrimp.
